posts.name: J0$hua Levin$0n
posts.cooked:
Separate from the 2022 thread, wanted to have a conversation specifically about what you are buying in 2022.

It’s a birth year for my daughter, so looking for a combination of things to lay down. Most, if not all, of the wine will likely be enjoyed by her parents but I like the idea of having some bottles to gift at 21 or 30.

My sweet spot in the past few years has been Canon, Lynch Bages, Brane Cantenac. Relative value matter to me and I am considering (but not sold) on splurging for 1-2 first growths since the year is special to me.

Starting this thread now because some reviews are out but prices/offers have not yet been released. So, what are you purchasing (or planning to purchase)?

posts.name: Max Swomley
posts.cooked: Brane has been my every year purchase, but if they increase pricing by 30% that will change…

posts.name: Justin S ! n g h
posts.cooked: It depends a lot on pricing. I will be a lot more selective if my usual favorites (Montrose, LCHB, Brane Centenac, DDC, etc.) end up being 30% more expensive.

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked:
Without pricing its hard to say for sure, but likely Canon, Carmes, maybe some Suduiraut. Dont plan on splashing too big, but if you’re thinking of splurging on top end wines, in your shoes I dont think you’d go far wrong with a case of Cheval Blanc, or 1 each of VCC and Eglise maybe. Of course, if you’re coming frmo the angle of “price is less relevant as its for sentimental reasons”, then I’d just get the opinion of Jeff/William/etc on what they thought was spectacular

If you asked me to assemble a parcel, I’d probably be looking at… Carmes, Canon, Clinet, Larcis Ducasse, Rauzan Segla, not sure which Pauillac off hand, Montrose, Leoville as a starting point perhaps?

posts.name: J0$hua Levin$0n
posts.cooked: Agree on pricing. Not to go off topic, but 2019 Brane has been available at decent prices. I grabbed a few this spring.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: Waiting to see pricing. Likely to buy VCC and Montrose.

posts.name: Hank Victor
posts.cooked: Most likely a little bit of Brane-Cantenac and Giscours.

posts.name: Ryan A.ns.el
posts.cooked: Great question- if it was my kid, based on my personal EP tasting and expected mid palate then it comes down to price… do you have a budget? Looking to go wide or deep or…?

posts.name: Ryan A.ns.el
posts.cooked: Ping me offline if you have questions on your wines from ‘22.

posts.name: J0$hua Levin$0n
posts.cooked: For me, in the past I have gone wide, but for the birth year I’d rather have 1 bottle of something spectacular than 2-3 still good but not as amazing bottles. But when it comes to 3-6 bottles of something like Canon vs 1 bottle of a first growth, I’d probably do the former. At the end of the day, I’m intending to drink/share these wines and am not focused on maximizing financial returns.

posts.name: Giacomo Buffetti
posts.cooked:
My little girl was born in 2022 as well.

Bordeaux has never been the center of my wine drinking and collecting, but since its age worthy, i’m eager to follow the advice of the many passionate connoisseurs.

I never fell in love with Bordeaux but i would still like to let her taste many fine examples when she comes of age.

Being life a funny humorist, once they’re adult most likely will not drink wine!

More for us then

posts.name: Ryan Whittle
posts.cooked:
I am guessing less than 4 case equivalents all for drinking. One will definitely be half bottles from likely Margeaux. I have really enjoyed Siran and it will be easy to convince me to continue that purchase.

I don’t see myself purchasing any standard bottles but I will be watching pricing and will likely change my mind.

I’ll be watching

du Retout (might purchased standards here. EP pricing for early drinking has been quite solid. Great large gathering wine)
Talbot (seems to have had a solid jump in quality and retail pricing has been high compared to EP)
Siran
Chevalier (expect to purchase magnums)
Chevalier Blanc (if released will also look at second wine)
Couvent de Jacobins (I have really enjoyed 2018 onward and think it is a great value)
Dry whites under $50 - pricing in the past has pointed to EP purchase verses buy when wanted but with the WK statement on them as a whole I expect to sit them out.

posts.name: Daniel Paik
posts.cooked: I’m leaning toward just tucking away my spending allocation in an interest bearing account

posts.name: Ryan Whittle
posts.cooked: Except for the odd formats, yes.

Of course, if you’re coming frmo the angle of “price is less relevant as its for sentimental reasons”, then I’d just get the opinion of Jeff/William/etc on what they thought was spectacular

If you asked me to assemble a parcel, I’d probably be looking at… Carmes, Canon, Clinet, Larcis Ducasse, Rauzan Segla, not sure which Pauillac off hand, Montrose, Leoville as a starting point perhaps?

Canon, Carmes, Montrose look solid so far.
Also looking at Rauzan, Clinet, GPL/Pichon Baron (depending on reviews, if the splurge is worth it), Troplong, LeoBarton/Poyferre (review dependent as well), and DDC. Usually try to spread out//not go too heavily in one appellation.

posts.name: Ⓑⓞⓑⓑⓨ Beachy
posts.cooked:
DanielP:

I’m leaning toward just tucking away my spending allocation in an interest bearing account

I’ve purchased some '19/'20/'21, but I think I’m going to focus more on back vintages finally. Nothing pulling me into buying immediately for '22 unless I can get a 20%+ discount via cash back site on something quality.

posts.name: P@trick Lynch
posts.cooked: Birth year for my first. It will certainly depend on pricing.

posts.name: Brian Befano
posts.cooked:
JulianD:

Canon, Carmes, Montrose look solid so far.
Also looking at Rauzan, Clinet, GPL/Pichon Baron (depending on reviews…

Exactly the same, likely to buy ep on the first three. Next three depends on price, but likely to wait on some combination and interest in verticals ranging from rauzan, ddc, gpl, leoville.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked: FWIW, half bottles are harder to come by these days. Glass producers are not making as many bottles, and corks for that size are harder to obtain as well. Plus, a lot of producers do not like to provide them. This is not for all chateaux of course, but it is a general feeling these days that half bottles are a lot of trouble. So, be careful when ordering them.

posts.name: J0$hua Levin$0n
posts.cooked:
@Jeff_Leve good to know on splits. I like getting e few mags (and 3L for birth years) since there is no premium on these en premier. But if they are going away I’m actually tempted to order more. Or at least a few half’s to track development early while the big bottles sleep.

On Montrose - I had the 2005 last year and it was a baby but just what I’m looking for. I’m worried about style change at the estate. Are newer vintages like the 2022 (or the recent 2020) on par with the 05, and any feelings on aging the current style? I feel like the Bordeaux estates are often like Fortune 500 stocks. We have our favorites, but it’s dangerous to assume past performance accurately predicts the current/future vintage longevity.

posts.name: Ryan Whittle
posts.cooked: Be careful because the quality of the corks? Or are there other issues?

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked: I would say for me, DDC is one that never really appreciates so I dont think you’ve got too much pressure to buy now. Rauzan, even 2018 which is supposed to be a really strong (potentially one of their best ever) cintages hasnt really appreciated despite being highly allocated on release. Clinet was fantastic in 2019, and I think they continue to do well, but they’ve moved price so quickly in recent years that there’s plenty of availability.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: In short, yes. 09-10 got a bit atypically big and fleshy, though still excellent, but then things tighten up again. Got a big vertical coming out soon in TWA which will go into what, when and why.

posts.name: JM San Roman
posts.cooked:
I have dared order (not knowing the price) Canon, Carmes, Troplong, Branaire Ducru (as per WKs recommendation), Leoville Barton, Larcis Ducasse, Tertre Roteboeuf, Rocheyron and Brane Cantenac.

My provider gives better allocation when I order in advance, hence the ordering before knowing the price. But I don’t think I will go wrong with any of these.

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
Well for me it will be my usual absolute year-in-year-out QPR wines from Durantou: Montlandrie, Cruzelles, Chenade, Saintayme and La Petit Eglise.

These wines represent more value than most anything you will find anywhere in Bordeaux.

Depending on where the pricing ends up I will certainly get a case of L’Eglise Clinet as it will outlive us all. An achievement.

Brane Cantenac will most likely also be a bargain for the quality.

Clos Lunelles was also another just brilliant QPR.

Suideraut and Lafaurie-Peyraguey from Sauternes.

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked: Bourgneuf out at £45 ($56). The 2016 is still available in the UK for £30.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:

Bourgneuf out at £45 ($56). The 2016 is still available in the UK for £30.

Slightly budget bound because I’m having my firstborn this year, so:

  • Carmes
  • Beausejour Becot
  • Laroque
  • Branaire Ducru
  • Giscours
  • Langoa Barton (probably)

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked: Yeah I picked up 750s of the 2016 for $42 on release. Would definitely backfill if I could still easily find at that price. But $40 pomerol may be a thing of the past!

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked: No cork issues that I know of. But it is harder to find smaller corks for half bottles. It is just a pain for the chateau to produce 375 bottles these days. There is not much interest.

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
Vince_T:

But $40 pomerol may be a thing of the past!

La Petit Leglise might very well get you there and it ages beautifully.

Clos du Clocher just might be around that territory and is an great value each year. Bonalgue from Audy stable is also good value.

You’ll not get a WA review but you will get great wine. Well, at least one for Swedish tastes.

posts.name: Y∅ñg £êë
posts.cooked: I’m a buyer of a bottle here and there only if I can buy them at a big discount by using promo/discount codes and cc offers.

posts.name: Ⓑⓞⓑⓑⓨ Beachy
posts.cooked:
martijnstolze:

Slightly budget bound because I’m having my firstborn this year, so:

  • Carmes
  • Beausejour Becot
  • Laroque
  • Branaire Ducru
  • Giscours
  • Langoa Barton (probably)

If I were you, I’d consider taking that budget and putting it in a CD until next year. Then, you’ll be able to decide the weight of your '23 vs. '22 allocation based on a better understanding of the '23 birth year vintage. I’m not sure those wines will appreciate much over the single year (but I really don’t know what I’m talking about there), and you’ll have 5% more of your '22 budget to spend (on whatever), unless you think any of these will be hard to track down a year from now.

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked: Personal view, Carmes if you can get the first tranche is likely to appreciate, but most of the rest of it is unlikely to over the next 12 months or so, esp with likely high release prices.

I appreciate this advice very much. However, I live in the Netherlands, and there the prices do rise signiicantly. There is no Bordeaux here which is cheaper than at EP. And next year, I will buy wine too. I don’t want to risk missing out on 22.

posts.name: Justin Izzo
posts.cooked:
I have one eye on EP this year, but if I’m honest with you all (and with myself), I’m probably going to backfill.
Thinking about picking up some 2016 Montrose, 2016 DDC, 2019 DDC, and some more 2019 Brane-Cantenac. For those who know “the market” better than I do, should I be concerned that 2022 EP prices are going to push up prices for back vintages quickly?

posts.name: Mont Stern
posts.cooked: We welcomed a granddaughter into our family in March of 2022 and I visited Bordeaux in April of 2022 so I will buy at least a bottle from each winery I visited plus my annual Canon purchase.
